data.title: MLN: Long Tenor Bonds And Interest Rate Risk
data.description:
We Need to Look at the Moving Parts

To understand how something works—to grasp what the output is going to be—we need to understand how the internals all work. This is true of any machine, and it's thus true of financial instruments as well. We need to know the construction, the moving parts, to see what the outcome is going to be in certain circumstances.

This sadly involves grasping certain background facts about the specific market. Which can be tiring but is, alas, essential.

VanEck Long Muni ETF (BATS:MLN)

This is an exchange-traded fund, and so when we buy, we are doing so from the fund itself. That also means that when we sell, our money is coming out of the fund, which imposes a certain risk. If many sell at the same time, then the fund has to liquidate positions. If those positions are illiquid, then they might have to do that at firesale prices - that then spooks others into selling, and we get a redemption cascade. Similar to a bank run, no one wants to be the last to arrive to get their money back in one of those. This ETF is in municipal bonds, liquid enough that this isn't a grand concern.

As an ETF, there are no borrowings to produce leverage, and also the fund won't trade far off net asset value—MLN has gone to near 1% off NAV, which is quite a lot for an ETF, but it's self-limiting, as if it goes any further than that, then it's possible to arbitrage the price difference. That arbitrage keeps the market price well within reach of NAV - unlike in a closed-end fund.

MLN is in municipal bonds, which requires a little explanation.

Bond Basics

Treasuries are lending to Uncle Sam. As there's been no bankruptcy there for a couple of centuries, we assume that these are risk-free. That's not quite and wholly true—governments have gone bust elsewhere. But that's a general assumption we do all make.

But that's also credit risk—there's also interest rate risk. This is that when interest rates change, so does the value of all bonds already in existence. If rates were 3% yesterday, 4% today, then yesterday's bond—to simplify—pays $3 per $100 per year, today's $4. These two bonds have different capital values—the value of $1 per year per $100 for however long the bond lasts.

Clearly that capital value changes more for a 30-year bond than a 30-day one. So, longer bonds (what we call longer "tenor") carry more interest rate risk than shorter. There's no real way around this either.

Which is a pity, as longer tenor bonds pay higher coupons/interest rates than shorter. So, to gain a higher interest distribution on our savings, we have to take more interest rate risk. Sorry, that's just how it works out.

Corporates are the second class of bonds, and we know that companies can go bust. This introduces credit risk, and therefore corporates pay higher coupons than Treasuries for the same tenor. Well, they do unless there's something very odd happening in the bond market. I do recall, decades back, IBM being able to borrow at a lower coupon than Treasuries. This was so unusual that it was front-page headlines (well, OK, front page of the business section perhaps).

Credit risk can be largely dealt with through diversification. We hold small amounts of many issuers. So, if one goes bust, then that's an annoyance, not a disaster.

We can buy, at issue, most bonds in $1,000 lots and get repaid at redemption. But the markets in secondary bonds—i.e., all the time between that birth and death—are more difficult. European corporate bond markets trade in $200k par value minimum lot sizes, for example. Being widely diversified in that size is going to be beyond most of us then.

The answer here is to be in a fund. The fund is large enough to be properly diversified; we own a fraction of a percent of the fund.

Municipals are the third class, and that's lending to subnational government in the US. States down to the local sewage plant stuff. These can and do go bust at times, but the default rate is really pretty low. The usual estimate is 0.1%. But that is credit risk, so diversification works. We also have interest rate risk and that painful reality that in order to increase our income, we've got to be in long-tenor bonds and so take more of that interest rate risk.

But given that munis should pay more interest/coupon than Treasuries, even if less than corporates. There's credit and interest rate risk there even if the credit risk is low. But they don't; munis pay less than Treasuries.

The reason is that the interest income from a muni bond is free of federal income tax (and for in-state issues, of in-state income tax for in-state residents/taxpayers). This means there's a weight of money interested in these bonds that then pushes the coupons down.

This also then gives us a technical problem, which is how do we value that income? For it's different depending upon the marginal ("on the next $ of income") tax rate of the investor.

Tax-Equivalent Yields

What we do is say, well, what would a taxable investment have to pay us to equal the value, cash in hand, of these untaxed ones? Clearly that depends upon the marginal tax rate of each investor, but fortunately people work out tables of this for us:

We can also lift the table from this specific fund:

The base 30-day SEC yield is 4.2%, but the one we more normally use for comparison is the 12-month—3.74%.

This tax-free income is worth more the higher the marginal tax rate. As we'd expect. But the general yield on munis is lower than other fixed interest markets. So, munis are advised against for nontaxpayers (and foreigners) and, to an extent, for lower-rate taxpayers. There's no hard and fast rule, but the breakpoint is, by rule of thumb, perhaps a 24% tax rate. Below that, well, don't bother. Above that, well, think hard about what you might be missing.

Back to MLN Specifically

Now that we have the basics—munis, coupons, and risks—we can see what it is that MLN is doing internally.

They're well diversified with nothing over 1% of holdings. Total issues held is around 600, which is also selective. The muni universe is vast, with one estimate at 60,000 issues (that, of course, includes multiple issues from one issuer). A long-held opinion of mine—an opinion, not a truth—is that index funds in munis don't quite work; it's necessary to be selective to gain significant results. This does not mean that those who are selective gain results, of course.

Fees here are 0.24%, which is higher than pure index funds. Sure it is. But fees, as fees, aren't the important thing. High fees for outperformance work, low fees for underperformance are a waste of money. How much cash we get after fees is what matters.

They've decided—or the fund is run to—minimise credit risk:

My opinion is that this might be a mistake. Again, an opinion, not a proof. But if the default risk in munis is very low anyway, then why not chase a bit of yield by being in non-investment-grade munis? As an idea, at least?

MLN is also in long-tenor bonds and therefore has long interest rate risk.

Years to Maturity 23.65

We can see the effect of that here:

2022 is when the Fed started raising interest rates again. So, long-tenor bond funds are going to get hit. It's simply inevitable that if the hunt for yield takes us into long-tenor bonds, we are then exposed to interest rate risk.

Which does give us a thought about MLN, which is, well, where are interest rates going to go next? If we think the Fed will continue to cut, then long-tenor bond funds will do well.

My reaction to that is slightly complex. In that I accept—have highlighted—that interest rate risk is an essential part of chasing yield. But I'm unconvinced of pursuing the opposite strategy of using an income fund to chase capital gains from falling interest rates. This could well be a personal failing rather than a useful investment technique.

Why I'm About to Be Wrong

If interest rates continue to fall, then a long-tenor bond fund like MLN will do well from capital appreciation. Just as it suffered in 2022.

It's also possible that I'm underestimating—even misunderstanding—credit risk in the muni bond market. Perhaps being near only investment grade really is important.

It's also possible that I'm wrong for reasons I don't know about—but I can't tell you those because I don't know about them.

My Opinion

While MLN is a perfectly sensible muni bond fund, I'm unconvinced that it works. This is not because the management is bad, or the fees are high, or anything. It's not even that the return itself is bad. It's that I think—my opinion, not my proof—that the strategic decisions don't quite work.

Munis are tax-free income; that part works. So, of interest to higher-rate taxpayers still looking for income. But in the details of the muni market, I think that—again, think, an opinion—the benefits of long-tenor bonds are overstated. Yes, we have to go long for income—or, rather, we'll gain income by going long—at the cost of interest rate risk. That part works here too. But I think that the credit risks of lower than investment grade are overstated. So going investment grade only in long tenor is picking up too much interest rate risk and, oddly, too little credit risk. Or rather, credit risk in munis is overpriced; there's an opportunity to gain income without having to take that credit risk. As I've stated here:

Now "not rated" in the bond markets usually means something like "complete dog and not touching it with your bargepole let alone mine". CCC isn't much better. Actually, in large parts of the bond market, BBB isn't thought of as much better than that.

At which point we could just assume that SHYM is achieving high yield by taking on lots of credit risk—the standard complaint is that we can only get paid in financial markets by taking risk.

However, the actual default rate in the muni markets is really very low—that 0.1% estimate above. So we could be dealing with comparative credit worthiness, not actual. Sure, a BBB muni is less creditworthy than an AAA but if the overall default rate is tiny, how much difference does that actually make?

Also, not rating means that the issuer hasn't paid a rating agency to rate the bond. This is not uncommon for small issues either. Fund managers can still decide upon their own opinion by having their own look at the finances of the issuer. That is, "unrated" does not mean "not worthy of a rating" it means "no one has paid for a rating" which is a very different thing.

Effectively, a fund heavily into unrated bonds is using the calculations of its own managers rather than the calculations of the rating agencies. Many funds won't touch unrated bonds, so there's a possibility that they're underpriced. Maybe, possibly, we cannot be categorical about this.

That, I think, is the special secret sauce here. Grasping that unrated in the muni market does not mean high credit risk, even if unrated (and other low-rating bonds) pay higher coupons. Thus, without a (substantial that is) increase in risk, it is possible to gain a higher income. Which is the great trick in any financial market—to get paid for taking risks you're not actually taking or to be overpaid for the ones you are.

I agree. That's an opinion. It's not a proof. But there it is, the opinion.

I think MLN, by limiting itself to investment-grade (near entirely) bonds, is missing that trick in the muni market. Which is why it pays less as a distribution than iShares Short Duration High Yield Muni Active ETF (SHYM). MLN is taking, seemingly, low credit risk and high interest rate risk, while SHYM is taking low interest rate risk and seemingly high credit risk. I think—to repeat, again, opinion—that's the wrong way to approach the muni markets. Given that credit risk is so low across the entire market, SHYM has it the right way around and MLN the wrong.

I think the base strategy at MLN is wrong. Thus I don't recommend it.

data.title: Record-High Price To Sales But Not For Equal Weight
data.description:
As shown below, the S&P 500’s price-to-sales ratio has climbed to historically elevated levels, recently hitting 3.33. For context, the dot com bubble peak in 2000 topped out at 2.27, and the post-COVID boom peaked at 3.21 before valuations pulled back.

While strong corporate profitability and the AI growth theme have gotten us to these levels, this chart highlights just how extended valuations have become relative to revenues.

But there's also a catch...

The equal-weighted version of the S&P 500 tells a different story, with its price-to-sales ratio currently sitting at 1.66, well below its 2021 peak of 2.18. This highlights the outsized role that mega-cap stocks have played in driving overall index valuations higher, as the typical stock trades at a much more modest multiple of sales compared to the market-cap-weighted index.

While still elevated relative to the early 2010s, the equal-weight P/S ratio is right in the middle of its 12-year range. While valuation excesses are concentrated in the largest companies, much of the broader market is trading at far more reasonable levels.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GBP/USD Forecast: Technical Breakdown And Key Levels Amidst Dollar Strength
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The British pound has continued its recent malaise against the US dollar after a stellar rally ahead of the US interest rate decision. Since the decision, Cable has been on a downward trend as the US dollar has continued to gain traction.

At the time of writing, Cable trades at 1.3343, down 0.77% for the day. The US dollar index, meanwhile, is up around 0.6% on the day to trade at 98.41 but faces resistance as price has tapped the 100-day MA.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart, September 25, 2025

US Data Boosts the Dollar

US data released today aided the greenback as it showed signs that the US economy is on a good footing. Weekly jobless claims dropped to 218 K, which is lower than the 235 K forecast and also below last week’s 232 K. At the same time, the second quarter GDP estimate was nudged up to 3.8% from 3.3%, which beat most analysts’ estimates.

August durable goods orders rose roughly 2.9% after a steep dip in July, while non‑defense orders rose about 1.9%. The inflation side of the report was a little higher too; core PCE prices ticked up to 2.6% from 2.5% in Q2. In some quarters, this may raise the question around persistent inflation despite Fed Chair Powell's recent comments.

Still many traders hesitate to place big bets before Friday’s August core PCE data, which likely matters a lot for the Fed’s next moves.

Fed Messaging Continues to Confuse

Looking at the data today, one could argue that criticism of Fed Chair Powell may be misplaced. The Fed Chair has been adamant on waiting for the data or a ‘wait and see approach.’ Last week's FOMC meeting saw the Fed cut rates but appear more hawkish than expected when it comes to 2026 and 2027.

This in part could explain the USD rally as the Fed outlook diverges from current market participants' expectations. The head of the Kansas City branch of the Federal Reserve, Jeffrey Schmid, stated on Thursday that current interest rate policy is "slightly restrictive," which he believes is the correct setting.

Schmid admitted that inflation is "still too high." However, he noted that the job market is mostly healthy and "in balance." He did warn, though, that new economic reports suggest there are "rising risks" to employment. This highlights the difficult balancing act the Fed faces: keeping prices stable while ensuring strong employment (the Fed’s dual mandate).

He concluded that the Fed’s recent decision to cut interest rates was necessary to reduce the risk of job losses. He stressed that the Fed is "close to meeting its goals" but must keep looking ahead to future economic conditions.

The back and forth between policymakers looks set to continue for now. Differing viewpoints are always encouraged, and that is what we are seeing from the Fed now.

Technical Analysis - GBP/USD

From a technical standpoint, GBP/USD has broken below an ascending trendline and gathered pace since. A potential longer-term correction toward the 1.3200 handle cannot be ruled out at this stage.

The RSI period-14 is trading below 50, suggesting bearish momentum. Immediate support rests at 1.3333 before the 1.3200 handle comes into focus.

Looking at a move higher here, the potential for a bounce could bring resistance at 1.3378 and 1.3500 (psychological level), respectively.

GBP/USD Daily Chart, September 25, 2025

Client Sentiment - GBP/USD

Looking at OANDA client sentiment data, market participants are long on GBP/USD, with 59% of traders net-long. I prefer to take a contrarian view toward crowd sentiment, and thus the fact that the majority of traders are net long suggests that GBP/USD prices could continue to slide in the near term.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Q2 GDP Third Estimate: Real GDP At 3.8%, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The U.S. economy expanded in the second quarter, posting a strong rebound from earlier in the year. The BEA's third estimate of real gross domestic product showed economic growth increased at an annual rate of 3.8% in Q2 2025. The latest estimate was higher than the 3.3% forecast and marked a significant turnaround from the Q1 final estimate of -0.6%.

Here is the opening text from the Bureau of Economic Analysis news release:

Real gross domestic product (GDP) increased at an annual rate of 3.8 percent in the second quarter of 2025 (April, May, and June), according to the third estimate released by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. In the first quarter, real GDP decreased 0.6 percent (revised). The increase in real GDP in the second quarter primarily reflected a decrease in imports, which are a subtraction in the calculation of GDP, and an increase in consumer spending. These movements were partly offset by decreases in investment and exports.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

Real gross domestic product (GDP) measures how fast or slowly the economy is growing and measures the inflation-adjusted value of all goods and services produced by the economy. It is considered the broadest measure of economic activity and the primary indicator of an economy's health.

The Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) releases real GDP data on a monthly basis. There are three versions released a month apart - advance, second, and final - each incorporating data that was previously unavailable. Economists can use GDP to determine whether an economy is growing or experiencing a recession.

This chart illustrates the annualized, inflation-adjusted change in U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) from the preceding quarter, with data beginning in Q2 1947. (Prior to Q2 1947, GDP was calculated annually.) For context, the chart also highlights recessions (as determined by the National Bureau of Economic Research, NBER), the historical GDP growth average of 3.20%, and the current 10-year moving average of 2.65%.

A key trend is evident: the 10-year moving average of GDP growth fell below the historical average in 2007 and has remained there, signaling a significant deceleration in U.S. economic expansion since the Great Recession. Current growth exceeds both the long-term series average and the 10-year moving average, indicating a faster pace of growth than experienced historically.

The chart above has many interesting data points but can be quite overwhelming to look at. This next chart provides a simplified look over the past few years.

Here is a log-scale chart of real GDP with an exponential regression, which helps us understand growth cycles since the 1947 inception of quarterly GDP. The regression illustrates the fact that the current trend, since the Great Recession, has a visibly lower slope than the long-term trend. In fact, the current GDP is 12.7% below the pre-recession trend (2008).

A particularly telling representation of slowing growth in the US economy is the year-over-year rate of change. Beginning in 1947, the average year-over-year rate at the start of recessions is 3.2%, with a range of 1.28% to 6.78%. The current year-over-year rate for real GDP is at 2.08%. The current level is at or below 9 of the 12 recession starts during this time frame.

GDP: A Look Ahead

In the chart below, we use the Conference Board's Economic Forecast for the U.S. Economy to visualize GDP forecast over the next few years.

September 12, 2025

Delicate State of Stasis

Higher tariffs are set to weigh on real GDP growth in H2 2025 and H1 2026 after already trimming growth in H1 2025, as consumers bear the brunt of higher prices. TCB estimates the bulk of tariff impacts will affect Q4, Q1, and Q3 of 2026. Fiscal policy is projected to only partially and modestly offset the negative impact from tariffs.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hertz: I Stick With A Hold Following Recent Debt Deal
data.description:
Traders are still deciding what to make of the prospects for car rental firm Hertz Global (NASDAQ:HTZ).

Hertz emerged from its bankruptcy in 2021, and shares were listed once again on the NASDAQ that November. The stock lost most of its value subsequent to that 2021 relisting as investors feared that the company remained too heavily indebted even after the bankruptcy reorganization. Some of Hertz's corporate decisions, such as a large investment in a fleet of Tesla (TSLA) vehicles, went sour resulting in further losses. These factors helped push HTZ stock down to as low as $3 per share.

All that changed in April when famed investor Bill Ackman announced that he had taken a sizable position in Hertz shares. This led the value of Hertz's stock to double almost immediately. Since then, however, HTZ stock has stalled out:

That said, after a summer swoon, Hertz has started to pick up steam once again with shares rallying from around $5 to as high as $7.54 in Thursday's trading session before closing the day around $7 per share.

A Debt Offering Sparks Trader Interest

The reason for the most recent jump came on news that Hertz is offering new debt which should help alleviate some of its near-term funding issues. That said, the pop faded quickly, and shares closed Thursday up just fractionally from where they were prior to the debt deal being announced:

Let's take a closer look at the debt offering and what it means for Hertz going forward.

On Wednesday afternoon, Hertz announced that it was planning a $250 million private offering of exchangeable notes. This led to the stock initially popping as much as 20% in Wednesday's after hour trading.

On Thursday morning, the company updated investors, noting that it had upsized the offering considerably, with the revised arrangement bringing in $375 million of proceeds.

Hertz will be using $300 million of that $375 million to realize the partial redemption or repurchase of its outstanding senior notes due 2026. It is spending $33 million of the proceeds on capped call transactions to limit potential dilution. And the remainder of the proceeds can go toward other debt payments or general corporate purposes.

These notes come with a 5.5% interest rate, which is a fine interest rate in isolation. However, the debt buyers are also getting a conversion feature, with the initial exchange rate being 108.2808 shares of common stock per $1,000 principal amount of notes. That would work out to a $9.24 per share rate if the bonds convert into equity. So, while Hertz is getting access to capital at an affordable interest rate, this could turn into a dilutive situation if shares move back to the 52-week-highs let alone into the double digits. Combining that with the fact that Hertz is spending a significant chunk of the proceeds on capped call transactions, and I don't especially love the terms on this deal.

It's worth considering that Hertz disclosed that Ackman's fund, Pershing Square Capital is involved in total return swaps on HTZ stock. The deal also includes capped call transactions with some buyers of this debt:

"Hertz Corp. reports that Pershing Square Capital Management engaged in cash-settled total return swap transactions.

These transactions give Pershing Square a long economic exposure to about $125 million of the Common Stock. The swaps have a fixed term of 36 months but can be terminated by the Swap Counterparty under certain conditions.

Hertz Corp. and the company have entered into cash-settled capped call transactions with some initial purchasers.

The cap price for these transactions starts at $13.94 per share, which is double the last sale price of $6.97 per share as of September 24, 2025, and will be adjusted as needed. These transactions aim to protect against dilution of Common Stock and offset potential cash payments Hertz Corp. may have to make."

Hertz needs more money to fund its operations and keep the turnaround story going until it can hopefully return to profitability in the future. These sorts of debt transactions are a necessary part of managing the balance sheet and giving Hertz more time to try to right the ship. Analysts see Hertz returning to modest profitability in 2027, so as long as it can keep pushing debt maturities out past that point, there is a shot at this plan working:

That said, the market's initial reaction, with a pop in HTZ stock that largely retraced itself, reflects the complicated reality here.

Hertz is managing a huge debtload, and as such, we could see more complex deals like this. It results in a situation where minority shareholders are along for the ride while larger stakeholders like Pershing Square or lenders can negotiate with the company directly. There's nothing inherently wrong with this; for example, we've seen Warren Buffett negotiate specialized investment terms for Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) when he has taken stakes in distressed companies. Just be aware that common HTZ stock holders may be subject to ongoing dilution from these sorts of swaps as the company tries to figure out to plug the significant holes in the balance sheet.

Other Recent Developments

I covered Hertz's Q2 results back in my August update on the company. At that time, I suggested that the Q2 earnings showed progress in terms of improved fleet efficiency, improved cost discipline, and a return to positive adjusted EBITDA. That was enough to move me from my prior sell rating up to a hold.

The company has not reported earnings again since that update, so there's little to add on that front. The other piece of Hertz-specific news was its partnership to start selling vehicles through Amazon's (AMZN) auto marketplace. That's an incremental positive for Hertz, though I don't believe it will be a near-term gamechanger.

In the bigger picture, it's worth asking whether the bullish thesis for Hertz is playing out or not. Back in April, Ackman suggested that the value of Hertz' vehicle fleet could rise considerably thanks to tariffs. In theory, as new cars became more expensive due to the tariffs, this would make consumers more interested in used cars and create a healthier pricing environment for secondhand vehicles. This would, in turn, drive up the value of Hertz's fleet.

It's hard to say what impact tariffs are having on the car market overall. A great deal of uncertainty remains as far as where tariff rates will ultimately settle and whether courts will hamper or outright block the collection of tariffs going forward, among other variables.

It seems, however, that the possible lift from tariffs is getting outweighed by other factors such as elevated interest rates and a cautious consumer spending environment. These facts came to a head on Thursday with used car seller CarMax's (KMX) earnings. The company missed both earnings and revenues dramatically. Shares plunged nearly 20% and are now near 10-year lows:

While CarMax has a different business model than Hertz, it seems that overall weakness in the used vehicle market could end up being a headwind for Hertz as well.

HTZ Stock Verdict

This week's debt offering is beneficial to Hertz, at the margins, in terms of giving it a bit more balance sheet flexibility. That said, it's a small transaction in comparison to the overall size of its balance sheet and debtload. As a reminder, Hertz had $5.4 billion in long-term debt as of last report and $23.6 billion in total liabilities. A $375 million raise is not much more than a rounding error in terms of Hertz's overall capital stack.

And the structure of the deal could potentially lead to shareholder dilution if the stock price does rally in the future.

As a result, this debt issuance doesn't meaningfully change my outlook for Hertz going forward. I was neutral on the company before, and I remain at a hold rating now.

My guess would be that Hertz shares ultimately trade lower. The business model remains challenged. And the company needs to show improved capital allocation and decision-making after prior blunders such as the Tesla EV investment. If a recession hits in the near-term, the pressure on consumer spending could really put a squeeze on Hertz.

That said, Hertz's market capitalization is not that high in comparison to its revenues or the size of its balance sheet. Shares are heavily shorted. Ackman remains involved on the long side. And there are outside factors, such as tariffs, which could in certain circumstances lead to favorable outcomes for Hertz shareholders. I wouldn't invest in HTZ shares here personally, but there should be more trading opportunities ahead. With both bulls and bears having reasonable arguments, a neutral outlook seems appropriate for the time being.

data.title: Micron Technology: Strong Buy As HBM Chips Power The Next Wave Of AI Growth
data.description:
Micron Technology, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:MU) stock surged 30.2% since my last report in late August, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 1.7% gain. Although the stock price dropped after the company released its fourth-quarter earnings, the stock has now exceeded my price target. With that in mind, it becomes even more important to assess the most recent earnings and the financial outlook to review the stock’s "Strong Buy" rating.

Micron Technology Posts Strong Earnings And Sales Growth On DRAM Strength

The reported figures were not extremely surprising, as Micron had already guided upward for the fourth quarter. That, however, does not make the growth in sales and earnings less impressive. Sales of $11.315 billion grew 22% sequentially and 46% year-over-year and were actually slightly ahead of the high end of the updated guidance range, which the company had put at $11.2 billion plus or minus $100 million. DRAM revenues grew 69% to $9 billion with a sequential increase of 27%. DRAM revenues rose due to a combination of volume and pricing. NAND revenues dropped 5% due to lower volumes partially offset by pricing. Overall gross margins expanded significantly to 35%, with operating income more than doubling year-over-year to almost $4 billion.

In the Cloud Memory Business, revenues grew 34.2% sequentially and 213.6% year-over-year to $4.54 billion, with operating margin expanding two points sequentially and 15 points year-over-year to 48%. Core Data Center Business revenues increased 3% sequentially but were down 23% year-over-year to $1.58 billion with margins of 25%, up five points from the previous quarter but down two points from the same quarter last year. Mobile Client revenues were up 16% sequentially and 15.5% year-over-year to $3.76 billion, with margins of 29%, significantly better than the 15% last quarter and the 20% in the same quarter last year. Auto and Embedded sales rose 16.6% sequentially and 27.3% year-over-year to $1.43 billion, with margins expanding from 11% to 20%.

The big driver of growth was High Bandwidth Memory chips, or HBM, which accounted for $2 billion of the sales, but we do note that overall DRAM revenues expanded strongly even without HBM.

Micron Technology Provides A Strong Guide For Q1 2026

For the first quarter of FY26, Micron expects revenues of $12.2-$12.8 billion, marking 10% sequential growth. That is lower than the 22% sequential growth we saw in the prior quarter. While the stock has been trading lower post-earnings, we note that analysts initially expected sales of $11.6 billion and have now lifted their forecasts to $12.5 billion, which, unsurprisingly, is in line with the guidance provided by Micron. Gross margins are expected to be 50.5% to 52.5% on a non-GAAP basis, a strong improvement from the 46% posted in Q4.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For Micron Technology?

The risks and opportunities for Micron are largely unchanged compared to prior quarters. The company has to manage its inventory well to prevent any write-offs, and by product group, it has to adjust capacity where necessary. The company has already done this by permanently decreasing its NAND production capacity where competition from China squeezes margins combined with a challenging demand profile.

The major opportunity is DRAM driven by data center growth. In 2025, the company benefited from the HBM3E shipments based on the 1-Beta node. The good thing is that for 2026, the company has already locked in most of the HBM pricing, leaving the company less exposed to sudden changes in demand. The next growth driver will be the HBM4 product based on the 1-gamma DRAM node. The company has achieved good yields for early production, which allows the company to start scaling production in the second half of 2026.

So, we have seen proactive actions for NAND, and we see strong progress and demand for HBM production and development, with a significant portion of the shipping volume already having the prices locked in.

Micron Technology Remains A Strong Buy, But Upside Post-2026 Does Not Impress

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Micron, I am maintaining my "Strong Buy" rating and adjusting my price target to $188.86. EBITDA in 2025 was slightly below the estimates, but for 2026, we see an upward revision of almost 28% and 12% for 2027, indicating a 20% increase in the EBITDA generation for 2026 and 2027 compared to the prior estimates. Doing the same for free cash flow, I observed that free cash flow generation was 30% lower than analysts had anticipated. Likely some of the 2025 expected cash inflow rolled into 2026 against a higher CapEx profile this year. That also bolstered the free cash flow estimate for next year. For 2027, the free cash flow has increased by almost 20% for a 27.1% higher cash flow generation in 2026 and 2027.

While I remain very bullish on Micron, we do note that the upside beyond 2026 is less certain. This is driven by lower expected EBITDA. There are two ways in which 2027 upside could be substantially higher. The first is if the trading multiple for Micron improves, which is not completely unthinkable given that it trades at a discount to peers. The other driver of higher fundamentally supported price targets is a higher EBITDA/FCF generation compared to what is currently modeled, and that would depend on volumes shipped, pricing, and rollout of the HBM4E chip.

I see significant upside, but do note that the company really needs to have higher EBITDA to continue justifying higher stock prices beyond 2026.

Conclusion: Micron Technology Remains Extremely Attractive

I believe that Micron Technology is executing extremely well, and while sequential growth will taper in Q1, the ramp-up of HBM4 should benefit the company in the second half of the year. Perhaps more important will be the demand and transition profile beyond 2026. That is where I currently see the first pressures, but it should also be noted that if data center spending continues to grow, EBITDA generation for 2027 could still be revised up.

data.title: Oracle: Its Valuation Is Not Traditionally Cheap, But The Shares Are Still Very Attractive
data.description:
What Oracle really announced, and why the stock price belies expectations?

Controversy is not necessarily a bad thing. Recently, in the wake of Oracle's (NYSE:ORCL) share price spike, investment opinions about Oracle have seen quite a bit of controversy. Some commentators on SA and elsewhere think the shares are now overvalued. Some commentators do not believe that its extraordinary backlog increase has relevance. In one instance, an author has expressed the view that Oracle's outlook is similar to that of the period in 2000-2 when the dot-com bubble imploded. One writer believes that there is risk in Oracle's major transaction with OpenAI.

This is an article that takes the other side of the recent sea of negativity. While Oracle shares are no longer cheap using traditional valuation methodologies, I find that using what I consider to be commonsense estimates, the shares are still exceptionally attractive. I am hard-pressed to understand why backlog and changes in backlog are not relevant here. Backlog, or RPO balances, is a key performance indicator for all software companies. While there can be some issues regarding duration, in this case, with the company having provided a specific revenue forecast to go with its backlog level, I would have to believe that backlog is the most relevant KPI. I have covered Oracle for a long time now-and that includes the period in which the shares crashed as the dot-com bubble unwound. What happened then and what happens now are far removed-they are not analogs at all, in my observation and opinion.

Customer concentration, considered in isolation, is always a risk. And I am not about to suggest, "This time it is different." What I do suggest in this article is that the deal with OpenAI has fewer risks than many and is likely to proceed as scheduled.

This is an article recommending the purchase of Oracle shares at this price and at this time. The company has transformed itself while holding on to the good parts of its legacy businesses. The management team has pulled off a rare feat in enterprise software and has emerged as a hyperscaler.

I have skin in this game. Oracle, after its performance so far this year and some recent purchases, had reached a portfolio weight for me of 45%. That wasn't prudent, and in the interest of transparency, I took action and reduced its weight to 30%. That is still a fair bit of concentration, and I may take further action. But I am waiting now until the company presents more financial forecasts, as it plans to do so during what is now called Oracle AI World. This is a customer and analyst event that will be held in the middle of October in San Francisco.

I also want to acknowledge that Oracle’s quarter was a miss and that the company will not generate high free cash flow margins while its growth rate for its infrastructure platform accelerates to an exceptional level. I am a great believer in the use of free cash flow margins and discounted present value in terms of looking at valuations. But neither am I a slavish devotee of one particular valuation paradigm. I believe Oracle’s business to be in a unique period of inflection, and using backward-looking valuation analysis will, in my opinion, produce exceptionally erroneous results.

Simply put, what works most of the time is highly unlikely to work all of the time. And what worked once may or may not be repeated. I try diligently to go where the facts lead without paying excessive obeisance to “how it is supposed to be done.”

For the most part, I am going to avoid a lengthy exposition regarding prospects for the AI market overall, although I think for some readers at least, a bit of commentary about context is necessary. I will say that I am a strong believer in the ROI of AI applications. Just the other day, a publication called Modern Retail ran an article in which it calls out that ChatGPT is now responsible for 20% of Walmart (WMT) referrals. I believe it is a revolutionary technology. Readers who feel that AI is hype will likely not find this the most comfortable of articles. I do use AI just about every day. It is a revelation for me - I think it works and works well. But as with many new technologies, it requires intensive management and some iterative processes until users are able to reap the benefits.

That said, and just as one single example, I challenge anyone to look at how Vibe coding works and maintain that the future hasn’t arrived. I am a strong believer in the Peter Lynch school of investing. Some swear by Graham and Dodd, others claim to be disciples of Warren Buffett, but I try to think about how Peter Lynch might approach the issue of AI investing. Mr. Lynch took over the Fidelity Magellan Fund in 1977 and remained its CIO through 1990. Over that span the fund had an average annual return of 29%.

Mr. Lynch had one particular paradigm that is relevant to this discussion. He would often pick stocks based on what his wife, Carolyn, told him was selling well in the supermarket. Sadly, I have no Carolyn to guide me through the maze of what is hot and what is not in the enterprise software world. I will have to settle for data on bookings. OIP is about as hot as anything I have ever seen in enterprise software-more so than the initial SaaS offering from Salesforce (CRM) and Splunk-now part of Cisco (CSCO), actually—and the sources for that assertion are far beyond just company co-founder and CTO Larry Ellison making the claim. When a hot product is animating orders, be it in peanut butter or IT infrastructure, take note and don’t try to explain that bookings are a distraction.

Finally, this is not meant to be a trading call. I have written more than a few purchase recommendations on Oracle shares for SA. I looked it up, and it said I have written 24 articles. The latest was on June 27th of this year, and before that in 2023, 2022, and 2021. I have held different size positions in the shares for all of those years.

There is apparently an agreement in which a consortium of companies, including Oracle, will be buying TikTok USA. In the last few days, Oracle shares have been sensitive to rumors about a TikTok transaction. Specific details of the transaction and the outlook for TikTok USA are not really known. I am absolutely not suggesting buying Oracle shares because of this potential transaction. Even if TikTok USA has annual revenues of $25 billion, as has been suggested, and even if the business under Oracle stewardship shows accelerating growth, that pales compared to the other opportunities the company has, which I will discuss below.

I can’t say that I haven’t traded Oracle shares, but my luck at that venture, except for the June recommendation, has been far from perfect. Oracle apparently has a fair level of retail investor ownership-24% according to TipRanks, many of whom have apparently been profiting after the shares have risen by more than 100% in the last 5 months. I don’t expect the shares to double in the next 5 months.

That said, Oracle, as mentioned, will be hosting AI World, as it now calls its annual user/investor event, in mid-October. At that time, Oracle will present some more specific financial projections for the next 5 years.

Beyond fiscal year '26, I'm even more confident in our ability to further accelerate our top and bottom line growth rate. As mentioned, we will provide an update on our long-range financial targets at our Financial Analyst Meeting at Oracle AI World in Las Vegas in October.

I obviously have no specific idea about what Oracle’s specific projections might be. The company has already provided part of the answer in its projection that OIP revenues will reach $144 billion by the company’s fiscal year 2030. Given that non-OIP revenues are already projected to be $50 billion this fiscal year, my own guess is that Oracle’s FY 2030 revenue projection will be close to $250 billion. Oracle has a current enterprise value of $965 billion, so I think the upside is fairly self-evident.

I am going to use non-GAAP numbers in looking at Oracle. There are those analysts who choose to look at GAAP, but essentially no one who deals with institutional investors does so, and has not done so for a long time.

Here are the key metrics that Oracle reported for this latest quarter: total revenues of $14.9 billion, up by 11% in constant currency. Non-GAAP earnings rose by 7% in constant currency, and non-GAAP EPS of $1.47 was up by 6%. Operating cash flow last quarter was a bit greater than $8.1 billion, up by about 10% year on year. Free cash flow was negative, compared to $5 billion in the prior year. Capex almost quadrupled year-on-year to $8.5 billion. None of these metrics in any way, shape, or form will justify Oracle’s share price, let alone a strong buy recommendation. And none of them have the slightest relevance for the future of the company-unless one believes that the company’s backlog and revenue forecasts are distractions and wishes to focus on the past rather than the future. For me, that isn’t just the wrong way to think but exactly the wrong way to think.

Oracle missed the prior revenue projection marginally ($100 million out of $15 billion). EPS was a miss of $0.01-$1.47 vs. $1.48. And that has literally nothing to do with a realistic attempt to value Oracle shares. Of minor importance would be the decline in total software revenue as on-premise customers shift to the cloud.

The guidance for the company’s Q2 results, while showing revenue growth acceleration, is, of course, not the reason the shares have appreciated. The headline revenue growth forecast is for 16% in constant currency terms. Non-GAAP EPS is expected to be $1.65, which suggests that the operating margin percent will be a bit lower than a year earlier. Full year revenue growth expectations were reaffirmed at 16%. While it is unprecedented for me to suggest that forward guidance for the balance of the fiscal year is not particularly relevant, the reality of the situation is dramatically different than anything I have seen before.

As is most often the case, Abraham Lincoln, in a far different context, got it right when he said, “As our case is new, we must think anew… we must disenthrall ourselves…” Of course the president was referring to something just a bit more important than how a software analyst might look at the valuation of Oracle shares, but the concept is the same. In order to reach the correct conclusion about the future course for Oracle shares, we really do have to disenthrall ourselves - a word I have never before used but one that resonates with me here after reading so many negative analyses of Oracle that miss the point entirely.

Recently, Bloomberg carried a story to the effect that Oracle and Meta (META) are negotiating a deal worth $20 billion for Oracle's infrastructure platform. It is but another indication of just how strong demand is for OIP.

Earlier this week, Oracle announced the promotion of two senior executives who have been responsible for Oracle's recent success. Clay Magouyrk and Mike Sicilia have become co-CEOs. Safra Catz has assumed a new role as Vice Chair of the Board. Their responsibilities will remain the same, but they will get additional exposure to investors as part of Oracle events and conference calls. Ms. Catz will continue her role as a senior executive working with Larry Ellison as part of the team of senior decision makers. The conference call held in conjunction with that announcement had little additional substance, and I won't further review it-the company did say it was meant to demonstrate its focus on its AI opportunity, although I doubt that anyone not living under a very large rock would be unfamiliar with Oracle's focus.

Also this week, there was a new release about the progress of Project Stargate. It is far further along than had been rumored, and new data center construction in an additional 2 sites has been announced.

The revenues from Project Stargate appear to be in addition to the revenue that is coming from the transaction announced with OpenAI.

What is the point specifically?

While there have been analysts who have chosen to contend that the growth in Oracle's backlog—from $317 billion last quarter to $455 billion this quarter—is a distraction, I would say it is the center point of any logical analysis. A year ago, Oracle’s backlog was $99 billion, as it had just started to rise significantly, growing 52% year on year at that point.

Why is the backlog important? Basically because Oracle’s newly named Vice Chair has used it to forecast revenues in the cloud infrastructure space! That is impossible to ignore or to claim it as a distraction.

The enormity of this RPO growth enables us to make a large upward revision to the cloud infrastructure portion of our financial plan. We now expect Oracle Cloud Infrastructure will grow 77% to $18 billion this fiscal year and then increase to $32 billion, $73 billion, $114 billion and $144 billion over the following 4 years. Much of this revenue is already booked in our $455 billion RPO number, and we are off to a fantastic start this year.

It would surprise me if this forecast wasn’t more of a floor - it aggregates $381 billion of revenue out of a current $455 billion backlog, and Safra Catz has forecast that the backlog will grow to over $500 billion by the end of the fiscal year. In other words, at that point (i.e., the start of FY '27), the company is forecasting that it will recognize $363 billion of OIP revenue over the following years through FY 2030 with a committed backlog of greater than $500 billion. That actually would seem to be a fairly conservative relationship of backlog to expectations for recognized revenue.

What is also relevant is some of the commentary that Safra Catz, now the company's vice chair, has made about the other parts of the business.

Now while much attention is focused on our GPU-related business, our non-GPU infrastructure business continues to grow much faster than our competitors. We are also seeing our industry-specific cloud applications drive customers to our back-office cloud apps. And finally, the Oracle database is booming with 34 multi-cloud data centers now live inside of Azure, GCP and AWS, and we will deliver another 37 data centers for a total of 71. All these trends point to revenue growth going higher.

I can’t say that Oracle reporting makes holistic analysis easy. Overall software revenue was down by 2% last quarter, while its cloud revenue rose by 28%. Software revenues are still 38% of the total, but that is down from 44% in the year-ago period. Cloud revenues, at 48% of the total, are now the single greatest segment of the company’s total. The other two components of Oracle’s revenues, hardware and cloud, still account for 14% of the total and are unlikely to grow at more than mid-single digits over a full year. The math to reach full year revenue growth of at least 16% self-evidently implies some kind of growth inflection in either cloud or software or both. The strong inference of the details presented on the call is that it will be both.

I am not particularly fond of the new revenue breakdowns presented in the income statement, and they can prove a bit confusing. While Safra Catz did present many detailed statistics, I don’t suggest that having some but not all of the revenue metrics in a specific category makes things easy. Oracle is still very much a database company, and the strong growth of its database segment will mean something when it comes to making some total revenue projections for the four years covered in the company’s projections for cloud infrastructure.

OCI consumption revenue was up [ 57% ] and demand continues to dramatically outstrip supply. Cloud database services, which were up 32%, now have annualized revenues of nearly $2.8 billion. Autonomous Database revenue was up 43% on top of the 26% growth reported in Q1 last year. Multi-cloud database revenue, where OCI regions are embedded in AWS, Azure and GCP grew 1,529% in Q1.

Some of these metrics, albeit that they are parts of parts, bode strongly for accelerating Oracle growth outside of OCI and outside of the exceptional deal signed with OpenAI, and the large deals with xAI, Meta, Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), and others.

Why has Oracle been so successful in ramping its cloud infrastructure business?

Larry Ellison has been one of Oracle’s leaders for a long time now. He is 81 years old, and in his career there have been high points and less successful periods. Oracle ran into financial problems at the end of the 1980s and secured an investment from Nippon Steel to provide needed liquidity. In the early 1990s, Oracle pursued a blind alley in trying to bring on-demand movies to consumers. In the database world, Oracle spent a long time denying the need for parallel processing in its flagship DBMS and lost significant market share, although it was able to reverse field and, as the linked article suggests, became the dominant RDMS by the end of the decade.

Oracle, like many other software companies, wound up selling substantial amounts of product to start-ups in the dot com world who were unable to pay for what they bought-although Oracle’s issues back then were far less serious than those of Cisco (CSCO), IBM (IBM), and its two most prominent database competitors, Sybase and Informix. In the later part of the aughts, Ellison downplayed the significance of the cloud and the SaaS paradigm, a decision that was very costly for Oracle and its shareholders.

I mention all of this simply because I am by no means an uncritical admirer of Mr. Ellison. In the day, I have shorted Oracle shares in the distant past, and I would do so again if I felt the arguments I am going to quote below didn’t resonate. But they do resonate, and it's a key factor in my Oracle recommendation.

I realize this is a bit long as a quote, but I think it is the crux of the argument-it is why those who are looking solely at Oracle’s most recent financials are using the wrong data and reaching what I think will be the wrong conclusions.

That's because Oracle builds gigawatt scale data centers that are faster and more cost efficient at training AI models than anyone else in the world. Training AI models is a gigantic multitrillion-dollar market. It's hard to conceive of a technology market as large as that one. But if you look close, you can find one that's even larger. And it's the market for AI inferencing. Millions of customers using those AI models to run businesses and governments. In fact, the AI inferencing market will be much, much larger than the AI training market.

AI inferencing will be used to run robotic factories, robotic cars, robotic greenhouses, biomolecular simulations for drug design, interpreting medical diagnostic images and laboratory results, automating laboratories, placing bets in financial markets, automating legal processes, automating financial processes, automating sales processes. AI is going to write that is generate the computer programs called AI agents. That will automate your sales and marketing processes. Let me repeat that. AI is going to automatically write the computer programs that will then automate your sales processes and your legal processes and everything else and in your factories and so on. Think about it. AI inferencing, it's AI inferencing that will change everything. Oracle is aggressively pursuing the AI. And we're not doing badly in the AI training market, by the way. With inferencing figure, oracle is aggressively pursuing the inferencing market as well as the AI training market.

Oracle 23ai is a vector database within the Oracle platform. What is unique, I believe, is that Oracle’s vector DB allows users to combine semantic search with traditional business queries. It also supports what is called RAG.

Basically, Oracle has become a leading vendor in cloud infrastructure and maintained a lead in the database area. I believe this is a unique combination not matched by competitors. Looked at another way, Oracle's infrastructure platform and its database offering work better together, and they have created synergistic revenue benefits that Oracle is leveraging now and that I believe to be underappreciated.

For any interested reader, here is a link that provides a far more detailed description of the attributes and benefits of 23ai. There are some real advantages to the Oracle approach. It is a single source of truth, it offers reduced complexity and overhead, it has built-in security and governance, and it offers the opportunity for advanced hybrid queries.

There are numerous dedicated vector databases on the market. The dedicated alternatives, such as Pinecone and Milvus, can produce higher performance and are better optimized for specific vector tasks. And of course not all users want to be locked into just one vendor. Typically, smaller users will find purpose built vector databases to have lower costs. As an aside, ClickHouse, 28% owned by Nebius (NBIS), can be used for Vector search. Many other companies, including hyperscalers, MongoDB (MDB) and Elastic (ESTC) have technologies that enable vector search. Most recently, both MongoDB and Elastic have highlighted their forms of Vector databases as a notable growth tailwind.

But I think Mr. Ellison makes good points in illustrating what Oracle has, how it can be used, and why it is differentiated. I have yet to read any real counterpoint to this take on the vector database market that identifies any specific Oracle vulnerabilities.

We think we are in a pretty good position to be a winner in the inferencing market because Oracle is by far the world's largest custodian of high-value private enterprise data. With the introduction of our new AI database, we added a very important new way for you to store your data in our database. You can vectorize it. And by vectorizing it, by vectorizing all your data, all your data can be understood by AI models. Then we made it very easy for our customers to directly connect all their databases, all their new Oracle AI databases and cloud storage, OCI Cloud storage to the world's most advanced AI reasoning models, ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, Llama, all of which are uniquely available in the Oracle Cloud. After you vectorize your data and link it to an LLM, the LLM of your choice, you can then ask any question you can think of.

For example, how will the latest tariffs impact next quarter's revenue and profit? You asked that question, the large language model will then apply advanced reasoning to the combination of your private enterprise data plus publicly available data. you get answers to important questions without ever compromising the safety and security of your private data. Again, I'd like you to think about this for a moment. A lot of companies are saying, we're big into AI because we're writing agents. Well, guess what, we're writing a bunch of agents, too. But when they introduced ChatGPT almost 3 years ago, what you got to do is have a conversation and ask questions. You didn't -- you weren't automating some process with an agent. You could ask whatever question you wanted to ask and get a well-reasoned answer with all of the latest and best information and high-quality reason go along with it.

Who's offering that to customers? We'll be the first when we deliver it and demonstrate it at AI World next month. That's what our customers have been asking for ever since the introduction of ChatGPT 3.5 almost 3 years ago. I want to ask questions about anything. And therefore, you need to understand my enterprise data as well as all the publicly available data. Then you can answer the questions that are most important to me.

Of course this is a commercial, Larry Ellison style. But that doesn’t mean it is inaccurate. Mr. Ellison presents a case as to why Oracle can do inferencing better than competitors.

Yes. Well, it's -- a lot of people are looking for inferencing capacity. I mean people are running out of inferencing capacity. I mean the company that called us -- I mentioned it, I think either last quarter or the quarter before, someone called us, we'll take all the capacity you have that's currently not being used anywhere in the world. We don't care. And I've never gotten a call like that. That's a very unusual call. That was for inferencing, not training. There is a huge amount of demand for inferencing. And if you think about it, in the end, all this money we're spending on training is going to have to be translated into products that are sold, which is all inferencing.

And the inferencing market, again, is much larger than the training market. And yes, We had to change our database, fundamentally change our database so you can vectorize all data. That's the form in which large language models understand information after it's been vectorized. And then allowing people to ask any question they want about anything.

And we -- that's exactly what we've done. But unless you have a database that is secure and reliable and linked to all of the popular LLMs, and we've done all of that, unless you have that and you have to tell me who else has that besides Oracle. Unless you have that, it's going to be very hard for you to deliver a ChatGPT-like experience on top of your data as well as publicly available data. That's a unique value proposition for Oracle. And that's because, again, we're the custodian of all of much more data than any of the application companies. They have their application data.

we're building -- like everybody else, we're building agents with our applications.

So we think we're better positioned than anybody to take advantage of inferencing

That said, there is one simple reason why Oracle has been able to close these amazing contracts that is at the heart of any realistic analysis of the potential for the company. Usually, at this point in an analysis, I typically include a section on competition and differentiation. In this case, I think the best discussion comes from the conference call transcript and is far better than anything I might present:

Mark Moerdler Sanford C. Bernstein & Co., LLC., Research Division

Larry and Safra and frankly, team Oracle, amazing congratulations. I'd like to focus on the AI training business you've been winning. Could you please explain to us how Oracle can create enough of a differentiated moat to assure this business does not get commoditized? And how do you continue to drive strong earnings and free cash flow from the training business even if training slows? I think people really need to understand that.

Lawrence Ellison Co-Founder, Chairman & CTO

Well, let me -- I mean, I can do it with one sentence. Our networks move data very, very fast. And if we can move data faster than the other people, if we have advantages in our GPU superclusters that are performance advantages, if you're paying by the hour, if we're twice as fast, we're half the cost.

I can’t independently evaluate the assertion, but third party analysis certainly suggests that the claim is not spurious. I have linked here to but one of many 3rd party analyses that highlight and validate some of Oracle’s performance advantages. Just in the course of writing this article, as mentioned, Bloomberg reported that Meta is considering selecting Oracle for a $20 billion OIP deployment for both training and referent processing. While certainly the OpenAI deal is by far the largest, there are many other transactions that seem to validate that Oracle has a significant lead in the AI infrastructure space. There are many articles that have been written about the performance advantages of OCI, and I have linked one of them here and provided a quote from a part of the article.

High-performance computing (HPC) and AI workloads: OCI's high-speed, low-latency cluster networking, built on a custom Remote Direct Memory Access (RDMA) over Converged Ethernet (RoCE) fabric, gives it a distinct advantage for large-scale GPU clusters used in AI and HPC. Oracle has partnered with NVIDIA to build some of the largest AI supercomputers in the cloud, and its MLPerf benchmark results showcase exceptional performance for certain AI training and inference tasks.

At the end of the day, the large customers buying OCI have done their own evaluations and have chosen Oracle because they believe they are getting material price/performance advantages. If they do, and third party consultants concur, I think it is a relatively safe bet that the assertion Larry Ellison has made is real, although, as with most things software, it is workload dependent and has lots to do with specific context. And as noted earlier, OCI performance advantages are greatest when using Oracle databases-one reason I expect that Oracle database revenue to continue to grow at elevated rates.

Valuing Oracle: You can’t look back, and using a traditional DCF analysis is difficult

I think valuing Oracle shares realistically requires thinking outside the box. No, this is not a call that “this time it’s different.” It is most definitely different, but I am anything but unmindful of customer concentration risks or the possibility that AI will prove to be a chimera. I do read the “Information” and other industry publications that seem to live on writing articles criticizing AI and OpenAI.

Starting off, no one buying Oracle shares today or tomorrow is doing so because of the company’s guidance of $68 billion in revenue, EPS of about $6.80, and essentially negative free cash flow. I find it surprising that there are commentators who actually think those metrics are relevant in any valuation analysis of Oracle shares, but as the saying goes, “De gustibus non est disputandum.”

I am sure that not all readers will find my methodology to their liking, and I expect that there will be those who find my assumptions optimistic. I actually think they are conservative, but as it is the future, proof points are sparse.

For the moment, I will take the $68 billion revenue projection that Oracle management has provided at its face. That is basically guidance for 17% year on year constant currency revenue growth, in which Catz has expressed confidence and commitment. Currencies are expected to be a 1% tailwind; hence, my estimate of $68 billion. With the company forecasting $18 billion in OIP revenues, this leaves about $50 million for everything else.

There are, essentially, two parts of everything else; one is cloud revenues, and the other is what Oracle calls software these days. Cloud infrastructure revenues were $3.3 billion, and other cloud revenues were $3.9 billion last quarter. Much of this was composed of Cloud database services, while another component was cloud application revenues, and a further bit was back-office applications. Using a 20% CAGR for this part of the business yields an estimate of FY 2030 revenues of $103 billion. That really depends on how fast Oracle’s 23ai database and the other pieces of Oracle's cloud offering will grow, but as I have tried to suggest, using OIP and 23ai together is simply the best way of delivering the optimal level of referent performance for users.

I am going to write something heretical here. There simply is no entirely rational way of projecting Oracle’s free cash flow margin in its fiscal 2030 fiscal year. There are more than a few issues. One I will call out in particular is that OIP revenues have a significant usage component. No one ought to presume to project usage growth over 5 years. Incremental usage growth is almost entirely profit. One would have to have the equivalent of second sight to guess the usage component of Oracle's revenue in FY 2030. It could be such levels as to produce 50% non-GAAP operating margins - I lean that way, but I really do not know.

Analysts trying to use the company’s current free cash flow margin as some guide to future performance are simply off-base. The faster Oracle is growing in 2030, the lower its free cash flow margin will be - probably. Basically, the formula for calculating a free cash flow margin for something like OIP depends on the percentage of renewals vs. the percentage of new installations in the mix. And no one, I believe, including company management, can really know how to model for that. It is basically 5 years from now. The senior executives are likely to be different (as mentioned, Larry Ellison is now 81, and although he shows no signs of slowing down, I have to wonder if he will be the CTO and company chairperson at 86). Safra Catz is younger, to be sure, but she and Mr. Ellison have had a close and at times personal relationship. They have run Oracle together with some other players, to be sure, for 26 years now. If there is a new management team, perhaps they will have different goals.

Probably the co-presidents announced this week will be key players going forward. They certainly have impressive resumes and seem on a track to be part of the management group of the future.

DCF analysis, which is a preferred tool in valuing the worth of a stream of future payments that the company has outlined, rests heavily on the terminal growth rate assumption. Most of the time a terminal growth rate is assumed to be in the low single digits.

I think that a terminal growth rate assumption past 2030 for OIP in the low single digits is highly unlikely, but rather than being presumptuous and trying to project a number without appropriate input, I will simply reflect that in a period of low growth, Oracle has achieved free cash flow margins in the upper 40% range.

After considering the issue, my best answer is that if Oracle is still achieving 20% revenue growth in 2030, its EV/S is unlikely to be less than 10X. And if revenue growth has fallen to high single digits with a free cash flow margin rising above 40%, then I think its EV/S ratio will be higher still. In other words, my projection is that a floor valuation at the end of FY 2030 would be $2.5 trillion (10X $250 billion in revenue). As mentioned, Oracle has a current enterprise value of about $965 billion, so my view is that its enterprise value will increase by at least 2.6X over the next 4 years. That is a pretty decent return when much of the revenue to support the projected enterprise value is already contractually committed.

The analysis gets harder if the time scale is compressed. Oracle is not going to generate free cash flow with $35 billion of capex, and my guess is that capex will wind up higher than that because demand is rising so rapidly. Here is a specific quote from the recent conference call.

OCI consumption revenue was up 57%, and demand continues to dramatically outstrip supply

I must confess that when I see so many negative articles about Oracle while some components of the company’s revenue are growing at unprecedented rates with demand dramatically exceeding supply, I am left to wonder. But regardless, almost inevitably, with growth at that level and with demand exceeding supply, the logical response is to ramp up capacity growth as rapidly as can be done with efficiency, and that likely means that capex, which was $8.5 billion last quarter, will exceed the current projection of $35 billion for the fiscal year. But then in that scenario, too, will revenue growth exceed the projected level of $68 billion, and the ending backlog, which I really think to be very relevant, will wind up to be well above $500 billion.

In the very short term, my guess is that Oracle shares may consolidate. Despite their recent pullback from the levels at which they traded immediately after the earnings release, the shares are still up by more than 21% in the last month and up by more than 80% on a year-to-date basis. There are investors who look at those numbers and take profits regardless of the outlook. I get that. The average price target as compiled by 1st Call is $333, with a high target of $410.

On the other hand, the potential for China and the US finalizing a deal for TikTok that includes Oracle is a short-term positive, and the potential for a $20 billion OIP order from Meta is an additional short term tailwind. If I were not constrained by prudent portfolio management guardrails, I would be a buyer rather than a reluctant seller of the shares.

Over the next year there will be many events to either validate or refute my investment thesis, starting with the investor presentation management will unveil at Oracle’s AI World. That is to say there are many opportunities for Oracle to prove its many naysayers wrong by continuing to execute and by benefitting from market trends. I do not present price targets; far too much of equity valuation relates to sentiment and the phase of the market. Oracle’s shares have become “risk-on” and their valuation will be driven by investor sentiment. I am recommending Oracle shares, and I do hold a substantial, overweight position. My belief is that Oracle will outperform both the S&P and the IGV substantially over the next year.

Risk to the investment thesis

I think the most obvious of the risks to the investment thesis is customer concentration. Oracle is a large company. And yet a single customer, OpenAI, is expected to represent about 75% of Oracle’s OIP platform revenues and perhaps as much as 40%+ of Oracle’s total revenues over the next 5 years. That could change, of course, if Oracle continues to book multi-billion orders from other potential customers. That kind of concentration is a real risk - and having seen the consequences of over-reliance on a single customer on past occasions, I am not about to leave the subject with an anodyne statement, “It's different this time.”

Of course, OpenAI is not a public company, so it doesn’t always present results or modify projections. As of July, it reported that it had reached an ARR level of $12 billion and was tracking to $20 billion by year-end. Actually, ARR increases seem to be at a rate of $2 billion/month most recently, and the metric has been rising. The company is projecting that it will reach revenues of $125 billion by 2029. That is the first year in which it expects to reach breakeven. It is projecting that its cash burn will aggregate $115 billion over the next 4 years. In March, the company raised $40 billion in its latest funding round with a valuation of $300 billion. Thus far, OpenAI has had a consistent track record of exceeding its revenue projections.

Most recently, a deal was announced in which Nvidia is investing $100 billion in OpenAI so it could accelerate construction of data centers with material incremental capacity of Nvidia GPUs. Presumably these data will host Oracle's OIP infrastructure.

Talking about OpenAI’s revenue projections would require a chapter length article. It is more or less equivalent to writing about the future of referent AI deployment. I think my Larry Ellison quotes on the subject were extensive enough. I believe the preponderance of the evidence supports that referent processing will be a dramatic chapter in the AI revolution with wide adoption by many different classes of users. That in turn means that OpenAI’s revenues will be more than sufficient for it to fulfill its Oracle purchase commitment.

I absolutely acknowledge this to be in the nature of a guess. It is one being made by many VCs who valued OpenAI at $300 billion in March. As of August, OpenAI’s valuation has reached $500 billion on a private exchange trading platform. There are many, many reviews of the Oracle/OpenAI transaction. Some of them are clearly clickbait in my opinion, and others reflect skepticism. Here is a link to a particularly negative article. Similar suggestions have been made by other publications and commentators. For the record, RPO, or backlog, is a disclosure required as part of GAAP. Presenting a misleading or false contract would be a crime and a violation of Sarbanes-Oxley. I have written and quoted at length about Oracle’s technology and the differentiation of that technology. Other vendors may seek to emulate what Oracle is offering and to compete aggressively on price. The argument that because “Oracle is twice as fast,” its cost is 50% below competitors is likely to be challenged over time.

As I mentioned earlier, the market for vector databases is crowded with many conflicting claims and assertions. As I wrote, I believe the concepts that Oracle’s strategy and technology in this area, specifically 23ai, are resonating with users and will continue to do so. That said, the database market has always been exceptionally competitive, and it is rare that one company has been able to achieve an extended period in which it has held a noticeable technology advantage.

Valuing Oracle after its most recent appreciation and modest pullback cannot be logically done using traditional valuation methodology. Oracle has no free cash flow currently, and its EV/S has reached an elevated level of almost 16X. I doubt that is a surprise to most investors, but it certainly has led many SA authors to publish articles with ratings downgrades. I have proposed a valuation framework that leads to a far different conclusion. Just today, the analyst team at Rothschild/Redburn initiated their coverage with a sell rating and a $175 price target. Their negative thesis relates to expectations that Oracle's deal with OpenAI will not produce the kinds of margins that other hyperscalers are likely to enjoy. I believe the argument is stretched, but some investors chose to act based on the thesis presented by the Rothschild analysts, driving the shares down 5.5% in trading on Thursday, Sept. 25th.

Oracle has become a “risk-on” investment. Indeed, at some level, it might be considered a proxy investment for OpenAI since shares of that company are not yet public. Market sentiment comes and goes. Right now, especially since the rate cut sentiment has changed, sentiment towards high growth IT vendors has changed as well. That won’t always be the case, but it is clearly a risk to the investment case.

Many analyst downgrades seemingly ignore Oracle’s differentiated technology and the resonance its offerings are having with customers. At the end of the day, that is really the basis for my share purchase recommendation.

Wrapping Up: Summarizing the case to buy Oracle shares

Oracle shares spiked after the company’s latest earnings report. While the share price is meaningfully higher, the path to sustained hyper-growth is meaningfully clearer. The company closed a $300 billion transaction with OpenAI, but it also closed several other multi-billion commitments from a mix of users for its infrastructure product. Its backlog has ballooned to $455 billion, and it has now projected revenue for OIP for the next 5 years, showing growth of 14X, to over $144 billion, or more than double Oracle’s projected revenues for the current fiscal year.

Oracle’s 23ai database is also showing exceptional growth and should start to move the growth needle higher. To support this growth, the company has ramped up its capex to $35 billion or higher. While some investors and commentators deplore this, I see it as a sign of long term strength. These data centers will be producing revenue for years to come, and the ultimate ROI for these investments is likely to be far into double digits.
