With the shares having pulled back by almost 10% from their post earnings high, I think this is a reasonable entry point. I recommend the purchase of Oracle shares at this time and at this price.

data.title: ClearPoint Neuro: Selling The Tools For Brain Therapies
data.description:
Introduction

ClearPoint Neuro, Inc. (NASDAQ:CLPT), caught my eye today. Its stock has rallied over 100% in just the last few days, and yet, there is no official news/press release from the company. Upon some research, I soon discovered that ClearPoint is trading in tandem with a partner, uniQure (QURE), following promising Huntington’s Disease (“HD”) data. I encourage investors to view the results themselves, provided in the following slide deck.

uniQure began trending on social media outlets (like X, formerly Twitter) and even made some headlines (e.g., ABC News) after reporting 36-month data in 12 patients receiving AMT-130, which is described by uniQure as an “AAV5 vector carrying an artificial micro-RNA specifically tailored to silence the huntingtin gene.”

In the study that compares AMT-130 with a matched external control arm, treated patients demonstrated a statistically significant 75% slowing of disease based on cUHDRS (p=0.003).

However, as I noted on X, although the data is supportive of regulatory approval, the path forward for AMT-130 is not set in stone.

With that said, the preliminary results are compelling, particularly in a progressive and difficult-to-treat condition like HD.

As I explore in the paragraphs that follow, this development really serves to highlight ClearPoint's differentiated offerings, meriting a bullish stance for risk-tolerant investors.

Recent Developments

ClearPoint, a small medical device company, has a device that allows drugs to directly reach brain tissue.

As noted in a 2021 ClearPoint press release, AMT-130 patients “underwent administration procedures using the ClearPoint® Neuro Navigation System and SmartFlow® MRI-safe neuro ventricular cannulae under MRI guidance.”

Although AMT-130 is unapproved (uniQure intends to submit a BLA in Q1 2026), and it is not yet certain if AMT-130 will be paired with ClearPoint’s device on its label, uniQure’s investigational use of the device bodes well for ClearPoint. In addition, ClearPoint already has an FDA precedent for device co-labeling with a CNS-related gene therapy (PTC’s AADC deficiency therapy, Kebilidi).

Kebilidi was FDA approved in late 2024 with ClearPoint’s SmartFlow cannula embedded in the product label. However, according to multiple sources (see link for one example), Kebilidi’s total addressable market, or “TAM,” is limited. Peak annual sales estimates are muted (“almost $275 million”). Notably, AADC deficiency is extraordinarily rare. Given that the drug will “take home” most of the sales, this leaves very little left for ClearPoint.

AMT-130’s TAM is much larger. According to the Huntington’s Disease Society of America, “Today, there are approximately 41,000 symptomatic Americans.” And as I alluded to in my X thread, many believe AMT-130 has the potential to be a blockbuster therapy. Subsequently, AMT-130 could become ClearPoint’s first meaningful revenue driver.

Modeling ClearPoint's Revenue Potential from AMT-130

The question then becomes: How much money can ClearPoint actually make from AMT-130?

Estimates vary, but ClearPoint disposables per infusion can be estimated at ~$15,000. uniQure is targeting 6,000 HD patients at launch. $15k/procedure x 6,000 = $90 million TAM. Obviously, this is a very basic estimate. In reality, AMT-130 may require multiple infusions within one surgery. Still, billing may be for one procedure.

This may not sound like much, but it is meaningful for a company the size of ClearPoint. Before the latest news, ClearPoint was valued at ~$300 million (market cap). With that said, given the nature of the procedure (one-and-done) and rarity of HD, the opportunity should not be misconstrued as a $90 million annuity.

As the company highlights in its most recent investor presentation, the opportunity expands well beyond AMT-130 and Kebilidi.

Of course, outside of its partnership with PTC Therapeutics (PTCT), these are all in clinical-stage development. Study and regulatory outcomes are very uncertain. Moreover, even after approval, gene/cell therapies often experience very gradual launches (for example, CRISPR Therapeutics’ (CRSP) Casgevy for sickle cell disease, which I’ve written on a few times in the past).

As far as I am aware, ClearPoint is the only integrated platform for biologics delivery at commercial scale. Its unique selling point is real-time MRI guidance. ClearPoint’s Prism provides laser ablation, challenging companies like Medtronic (MDT). Prism was just cleared by the FDA to “include 1.5T MRI guidance in addition to the previously cleared 3T MRI guidance.” Although Prism expands the TAM, ClearPoint’s clearest differentiator is its emerging role as a “combination product” (device+drug labeled together), and I think its stock rally following AMT-130 really speaks to this.

Financial Health

Turning to earnings, ClearPoint generated $9.2 million in revenue in Q2 2025 (+17% Y/Y). This is split up between product ($5.997 million) and service ($3.218 million). Cost of revenue was $3.659 million, which was good for a gross margin of 60%. Notably, this decreased by 300 basis points (3%) Y/Y due to “higher excess and obsolete inventory reserves.” Following operating expenses (“OpEx”) like R&D and S&M, ClearPoint’s operating loss was $5.68 million, which narrowly expanded compared to last year ($4.724 million).

It’s important to consider that ClearPoint is still in the early innings of scaling its business. With that said, following progress in its “four pillar growth strategy,” which is laid out in its investor presentation, the company expects to expand its margin to 70% and achieve cash flow breakeven by the end of 2027. Looking ahead to FY 2025, management guided for between $36 million and $41 million.

As for cash, ClearPoint had $41.5 million as of June 30, 2025. Cash from operations (“cash burn”) was $8.8 million in the first half of this year ($17.6 million annualized cash burn). This gives ClearPoint ~2 solid years of “cash runway.” The company does have access to up to $75 million in additional debt and $50 million in an unused ATM facility, which would effectively extend its cash runway. The company’s last public offering was in March 2024. So, there is a potential for another raise/dilution.

ClearPoint’s debt (principal) is $30.2 million on the Oberland First Purchase Note. No principal payments are required until maturity in mid-2031. I think this is a reasonable amount of debt for ClearPoint (no other loans; debt/TTM revenue ~0.9).

Conclusion

To wrap up, AMT-130 data, limitations aside, point to ClearPoint’s large potential in the drug delivery device market, where the company holds an edge in CNS applications. Moreover, I think the company appears well-positioned to make good on its goals. Critically, recent funding gives it the “firepower” needed to take it to the next level (profitability).

With that said, although the recent stock rally implies immediate gains from AMT-130 developments, ClearPoint’s path moving forward may be painstakingly gradual and dependent on uncertain outcomes like regulatory decisions and gene therapy launches.

For what it is (a small-cap medical device company), I think CLPT is attractive (“Buy”) due to its optionality and opportunity, particularly in a barbell portfolio that limits too much allocation. Personally, as per my framework, I would wait for the “dust to settle” for another day or two before adding/initiating a position, as I wouldn’t want to risk trading into volatility/ATM overhang.

data.title: Victoria's Secret & Co.: Very Solid Improvements, But Patience Is Still Needed
data.description:
Investment action

I gave a hold rating for Victoria’s Secret & Co. (NYSE:VSCO) previously, as I did not see a strong catalyst to justify upgrading to a buy, especially with the stock valuation at the 5-year average. Fast forward to today, and I believe the setup has improved. The core intimates category has shown signs of recovery, PINK and Beauty are gaining traction, and international growth is accelerating. These developments fundamentally de-risk the story, but I think patience is still needed. I reiterate a hold rating as I await more clarity on the pace of turnaround.

The Core Is Healing, And Customers Are Taking Notice

I was previously leaning bearish because the brand was steadily losing share in the very market it once was a leading player in. Things have changed for the better, and there are numbers to back this. VSCO gained ~0.5 points of market share in total bras during 2Q25 and expanded its share in the broader intimates category among the critical 18–44 demographic. While it is still too early to call it a recovery, this is a very encouraging signal that VSCO is starting to play offense. And I say this because of the launch of the new FlexFactor bra. For the first time in years, a single bra introduction has managed to lift sales across the entire bra category. This is important because it shows that VSCO's revitalized product and marketing engine is working, and notably, it is generating momentum beyond one SKU and is driving consumers back to the brand. We know that consumers are coming back to the brand since total active customers increased 5% in July, and these are from new, existing, and reactivated shoppers. Even better, one of the fastest-growing cohorts was the 18–24 age group, which is another strong validating point that VSCO can resonate with younger consumers (effectively de-risking the longer-term growth outlook).

For a brand that is in the turnaround phase, this is absolutely bullish. The bottom line here is that the most important part of the VSCO business is no longer a liability. The core business is showing real signs of healing and is de-risking the equity story.

Other growth drivers

Aside from the core intimates business, VSCO’s other growth engines are also showing real momentum.

Firstly, it’s on PINK. I noted before that PINK was an early green shoot, and it appears that I was right about PINK. This brand has matured nicely, and while PINK sales grew in the low single digits overall, the quality of this growth is where attention should be paid. Within PINK, apparel saw a double-digit increase, driven by higher full-price sell-through, which proved that demand is being driven by organic demand strength rather than markdowns. The PINK x LoveShackFancy collaboration was a major validating data point. This collaboration was done with zero discounting, and both brands saw record sales, with the top-selling item being a $200 Letterman Jacket. I would think that this kind of pricing power is a clear signal of brand heat.

Secondly, it’s the Beauty segment. Momentum here is not losing steam as well, as it has delivered its eighth consecutive quarter of growth in 2Q25, with sales up mid-single digits. The strength was also broad-based, with growth across all major categories, including body care, seasonal fragrance, and the mist collection, the last of which saw low-teens growth. What is important here is the strategic opportunity Beauty represents. The way I see it, it serves as an accessible entry point to the VSCO brand, especially for teenagers or younger consumers that do not have the buying power to purchase the intimates category but still want to own a VSCO product. As much as the core business for VSCO is intimates, I thought it made sense for VSCO to ramp up investments in the beauty market since the opportunity set is so lucrative. For perspective, the beauty market is roughly four times the size of the intimates market in the US (source: 2Q25 earnings transcript), and with VSCO holding only a single-digit share (source: Goldman Sachs 32nd Annual Global Retailing Conference), there is clearly a massive runway for growth. Furthermore, only <40% of VSCO's existing customers currently shop for beauty, which means there is a significant opportunity to cross-sell and increase the lifetime value of VSCO's customer base.

Valuation

In my view, the path to higher earnings has definitely become clearer. Unlike my write-up in June, the fundamentals and growth outlook look a lot better today. The core intimates category has inflected, and the other growth drivers are seeing momentum. Both of which fundamentally de-risk the story. I should also note that if readers paid attention to the sequential improvements in the 2Q25 results, they would have noticed that comparable sales growth [CSG] accelerated by 500 bps sequentially from -1% in 1Q25 to 4% in 2Q25. VSCO also managed to massively beat its own EPS guidance, reporting adjusted EPS of $0.33, well ahead of guidance for $0.00 to $0.15.

The bull case here is that VSCO growth has really inflected (i.e., 2Q26 is the inflection point), and earnings growth from here should stay elevated for the foreseeable future. This would justify VSCO to trade at a premium to its average multiple of 9x (now at 12x forward P/E). It could potentially go even higher to 14x (the +2 standard deviation level) if momentum gets stronger in my opinion.

However, the bear case is that this recovery trend fails to sustain. The core category is not improving as well as it should, and VSCO fails to see growing traction with beauty and PINK. These would absolutely weigh on the multiples, potentially dragging it back to as low as 6x forward P/E (low in April this year).

My view is leaning towards the bull case, but with caution. I recognized the turnaround potential here, but I do think it is wiser to wait for a few more quarters so that we can get more clarity on the pace of recovery (is it accelerating, slowing, or stopping?). Once we get clarity that the core category is indeed improving, and there are no slowdowns in momentum elsewhere, I don’t think it will be too late to get on board this stock. Hence, for now, I still reiterate a hold rating.

Conclusion

At this stage, I still lean toward patience. The core intimates recovery and strong performance in PINK and Beauty are highly encouraging, but sustainability is key. If VSCO can prove that momentum is durable across multiple categories for a few more quarters, the risk-reward would look much more compelling. For now, valuation sits at ~12x forward earnings, already above historical averages, and I believe a hold rating remains appropriate until we have greater visibility on the turnaround trajectory.

data.title: Intel: Non-Core Asset Monetization Is A Win For Investors
data.description:
I do not believe in timing the market. In hindsight, however, I couldn't have invested in Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) at a better time. A little over a year ago, I turned bullish on INTC for the first time in many years after digesting the progress of then-CEO Pat Gelsinger's turnaround plan. Three months later, I struck a more cautious note but continued holding the shares I invested in, as I thought Intel could continue to make progress in the right direction. Since my first bullish call, Intel stock is up ~60%, making it one of the best-performing stocks in my portfolio over the past 12 months. Today, under new CEO Lip-Bu Tan, I believe Intel is at an important juncture.

In my previous analysis, I raised concerns regarding the lackluster monetization of Intel's non-core assets. Under the new CEO, Intel seems to be headed in the right direction to monetize these assets to support investments in the core business.

Monetization Status Of Non-Core Assets

Before I delve deeper into Intel's business, I believe it is important to highlight Intel's new execution strategy under CEO Tan. As illustrated below, in its Q2 presentation, Intel highlighted monetizing non-core assets as a key strategic step to improve its balance sheet health.

Exhibit 1: Intel's strategic priorities

To provide more context, Intel's core business is its IDM model, which focuses on designing and manufacturing processors. Historically, this core business included the fabless operations, but given how the business has changed over the years, I am considering Intel Foundry as part of its core business as well. In my opinion, this makes sense given that the Foundry business is strategically important to the flawless execution of the IDM 2.0 strategy. It is encouraging that Intel is increasingly trying to manage the Foundry business as a standalone unit, which should help this business unit operate with better efficiency and financial discipline.

In addition to this core business, Intel owns a few non-core assets.

  1. Altera
  2. Mobileye
  3. IMS Nanofabrication
Asset Business line Monetization status
Altera This is the programmable solutions business that develops FPGAs. Last April, Intel decided to sell a 51% stake of this business unit to Silver Lake Partners for $4.4 billion in net cash and $1 billion in deferred payments. The deal values Altera at $8.75 billion, significantly lower than the $16.6 billion valuation at which Intel invested in Altera in 2016.
Mobileye This is the business unit that designs and develops self-driving technologies and sensor systems. Intel monetized this investment in 2022 with the IPO of Mobileye. However, Intel still has a majority stake in Mobileye. A secondary offering was completed in July as well.

IMS Nanofabrication

 This is Intel's multi-beam mask writing tool business, which is based in Austria. Intel has so far shown no interest in monetizing its majority ownership in IMS.

Source: Company Filings

The new CEO made his intentions clear during the Q2 earnings call, opening up the possibility of further monetization opportunities in the future.

Lastly, as it relates to our noncore assets, we successfully monetized a portion of our ownership of Mobileye's earlier this month, and we look forward to closing the Altera transaction with Silver Lake this quarter. I will evaluate other opportunities as we continue to sharpen our focus around our core business and strategy. I believe the actions we have taken during my first few months are steering us in the right direction.

CFO David Zinsner also referenced how Intel is taking steps to focus on its core business while freeing up capital from its non-core assets.

In Q2, we took tangible steps to increase focus on our core business while leveraging non-core assets to shore up the balance sheet.

With this understanding of the current status of Intel's non-core asset monetization strategy, the next step is to evaluate how this impacts Intel's business and valuation.

The Expected Business And Valuation Impact Of Monetizing Non-Core Assets

The most important business impact will be Intel's ability to redirect time and money into its core business. This should help the company's efforts to simplify its business. Tan, since joining the company, has highlighted the importance of simplifying Intel's complex business model, which, in my opinion, is the right move given how operational complexities have led to inefficient capital allocation and strategic priority setting in the past.

Intel's balance sheet will see an immediate boost as a result of these monetization efforts. From the sale of the Altera stake, Intel will receive $4.4 billion in cash, and the company has already collected ~$920 million in net proceeds through the secondary offering of Mobileye shares last July. In the first quarter, Intel received the final proceeds of ~$2 billion from the sale of its NAND business (the first part of the sale of the NAND business to SK hynix was completed in December 2021). This massive flow of non-dilutive cash received through the monetization of non-core assets will not only strengthen Intel's balance sheet but will also help the company fund capital investments without tapping debt markets.

In addition to this, I believe the divestiture of capital-intensive non-core assets will help the company see a permanent reduction in CapEx requirements. For the current fiscal year, Intel has lowered its CapEx guidance to $18 billion from projections for around $23 billion at the beginning of the year. There is evidence to suggest that this is likely to be the beginning of a new trend in CapEx. On the Q2 earnings call, CFO Zinsner said:

Better utilization of our construction in progress will allow us to deploy more overall CapEx in 2025 than in 2024, and we expect the improved utilization to continue in 2026, resulting in lower gross and net CapEx next year. Beginning the process of delevering our balance sheet in 2025 remains a top priority for us.

Exhibit 2: Intel's capital expenditures

The monetization of non-core assets will help the company's balance sheet health by removing goodwill associated with these assets as well. For instance, the sale of its 51% stake in Altera will remove goodwill and other intangible assets associated with Altera from Intel's balance sheet, as the remaining 41% stake will be accounted for as an equity investment. I believe this would help Intel have a much cleaner balance sheet with a high proportion of its assets representing tangible assets.

With the sale of a majority stake in Altera, Intel's non-GAAP EPS will no longer account for Altera's financial performance. Instead, Altera's contribution will be shown as a gain/loss from equity investments. I believe this is a not-so-often talked about positive impact that stems from Intel's non-core asset monetization strategy. Moving forward, Intel's non-GAAP EPS will act as a good proxy for the company's core business performance. Also, the All Other reporting segment, in which Altera's financial performance was grouped, reported an operating margin of just 6.6% in Q2 compared to 23% for the Intel Products segment. Therefore, removing the Altera business from non-GAAP EPS should be immediately accretive to Intel's non-GAAP operating margins.

From a valuation perspective, monetizing non-core assets is likely to lead to a few positive outcomes.

  1. A cleaner, healthier balance sheet.
  2. Improved profitability metrics.
  3. Lower CapEx.
  4. Aggressive investments in the core, profitable business.
  5. A reduction in operating expenses (according to filings, management layers have been cut in half already).

In summary, I believe non-core asset monetization will make Intel a leaner, operationally efficient, and financially healthier organization that is well-positioned to benefit from regulatory tailwinds.

Takeaway

Intel has a lot to prove. Risks facing Intel are well-documented, which include the threat of disruption by chipmakers that have surpassed Intel's technological capabilities in the past decade. My investment thesis for Intel is centered around the company's potential to capture a meaningful share of the AI chip market by aggressively investing in its core business. I believe CEO Tan's focus on monetizing non-core assets bodes well for my thesis. However, given the many uncertainties facing Intel's core business, such as intensifying competition and technological inferiority, I will continue to rate Intel shares a hold for now. While I am not planning to add to my long position, I am not planning to book the gains from my INTC investment either.
